one_line_takeaway: A learning-theory paper proving that, in an online model where an adversary secretly injects clean-labeled points into an i.i.d. stream, not knowing the underlying data distribution costs a provably tight Omega(sqrt(T)) additional error even for VC-dimension-1 classes, and introduces a new 'certificate dimension' notion that yields the first sublinear distribution-agnostic learnability bound (tilde-O(T^{2/3})) for 2D halfspaces under this adversarial-injection/abstention model.
key_finding: The central result is a proved gap between two information regimes for this online abstention model: without access to the underlying distribution D, even the simplest non-trivial concept class (VC dimension 1) forces Omega(sqrt(T)) expected combined error for every learner (Theorem 3.1) -- and this exactly matches the prior Otilde(sqrt(T)) upper bound of Goel et al. 2024a, versus the O(d^2 log T) rate achievable with oracle access to D -- so the paper proves the gap is real and tight rather than an artifact of loose analysis. Algorithmically, the new certificate-dimension notion lets the paper give the first sublinear (distribution-agnostic) bound for 2D halfspaces under adversarial injection, tilde-O(T^{2/3}), which the authors juxtapose with Blum et al. 2021's result that 2D halfspaces are NOT robustly learnable under clean-label poisoning at all when abstention is disallowed -- illustrating, within the paper's own theoretical framework, that allowing abstention changes learnability, not that abstention is a general practical defense against poisoning.
core_contribution: A learning-theory paper on the 'adversarial injection' online learning model (Goel et al. 2024a), where a stream of clean-labeled examples is mostly i.i.d. from a distribution D but an adversary can inject arbitrary (still correctly-labeled) points on rounds the learner cannot identify, and the learner may abstain instead of predicting. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a tight Omega(sqrt(T)) lower bound on expected combined error (misclassifications + charged abstentions) for distribution-agnostic learners even at VC dimension 1, proved via a hard rooted-tree concept class and Yao's minimax principle, which matches the prior Otilde(sqrt(T)) upper bound and establishes a sharp separation from the O(d^2 log T) rate achievable when the learner knows D; (2) a new potential-based algorithmic framework (leave-k-out potentials, 'robust witnesses,' (m,c,k)-resilient score functions, and a generic 'Algorithm 2' learner) that unifies and generalizes prior distribution-agnostic algorithms, with a general combined-error bound (Theorem 4.3); (3) a new combinatorial notion, 'certificate dimension' (Definition 5.3), a finite-alphabet relaxation of the pre-existing 'inference dimension' (Kane et al. 2017) tailored to this framework; and (4) using certificate dimension, the first distribution-agnostic upper bound for halfspaces in R^2, Otilde(T^{2/3}) (certificate dimension 3), improving on the Otilde(T^{4/5}) bound obtainable from inference dimension (halfspaces in R^2 have inference dimension 5, a fact from Kane et al. 2017, not this paper).
threat_model: Not a systems/empirical threat model -- this is an abstract online-learning adversary internal to the paper's own protocol (Protocol 1). On each of T rounds an adversary picks a hidden bit q_t: if q_t=0, nature draws x_t i.i.d. from an unknown, fixed distribution D; if q_t=1, the adversary injects an arbitrary x_t of its choosing. Labels are always truthful w.r.t. a fixed target concept c* (the 'clean-label' setting -- no label-flipping). The learner sees x_t, must predict a label or abstain, then observes the true label y_t, and is never told whether the round was i.i.d. or injected; the injecting adversary may be adaptive (choice of q_t and x_t can depend on history). Loss (combined error) counts every misclassification plus every abstention that occurred on an i.i.d. round (abstaining on an injected round is free). This is presented by the authors as the online analogue of clean-label data-poisoning robustness (citing Shafahi et al. 2018 'Poison Frogs' and Blum et al. 2021) and of PQ-learning/testable-distribution-shift settings, but the paper itself contains no real dataset, system, or model under attack -- it is entirely a worst-case combinatorial/statistical analysis.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors in the Discussion section: (1) the Omega(sqrt(T)) separation lower bound uses only an oblivious adversary; whether adaptive injection strategies force even larger error for other classes is left open. (2) The abstention upper bounds (both this paper's and Goel et al. 2024a's) rely fundamentally on exchangeability of the i.i.d. samples; the authors note this technique may not be the right tool if the adversary or learner's information is restricted differently, and controlling charged abstentions without exchangeability is an open problem. (3) Halfspaces in R^d for d>=3 remain open -- inference dimension is infinite in higher dimensions, and it is unclear whether this is a fundamental barrier of the injection model or merely a limitation of inference/certificate-dimension-based proof techniques; the authors flag even O(T^{1-1/(d+1)}) as an open target. (4) No single combinatorial parameter is known to characterize learnability/rates in this model the way VC dimension does for stochastic learning or Littlestone dimension for fully adversarial online learning -- the authors state this as a central open problem. (5) The R^2 halfspace certificate-dimension result requires the learner to start with at least one sample of each label (footnote 5), handled by a minor bootstrapping argument (at most two mistakes, no abstentions) rather than being fully general from round 1. No empirical validation of any bound is presented anywhere in the paper.

quantitative_results: Omega(sqrt(T)) expected combined error lower bound for distribution-agnostic learners on a VC-dimension-1 concept class (Theorem 3.1, this paper's own result)
Matches the prior Otilde(sqrt(T)) distribution-agnostic upper bound for VC-1 from Goel et al. 2024a, establishing a tight separation from the O(d^2 log T) known-D rate (also Goel et al. 2024a)

General framework bound: errmis <= c/(k! alpha) * T^k and E[errabs] <= em(alpha + c T^{k-1})^{1/k} log T, balancing to combined error tilde-O(c^{1/(k+1)} (mT)^{1-1/(k+1)}) (Theorem 4.3, this paper's own framework)

Axis-aligned rectangles in R^d: combined error tilde-O(d sqrt(T)) recovered as a special case of the paper's framework ((2d+1,d,1)-resilient function, Lemma 4.6) -- this rate itself originates in Goel et al. 2024a, the paper shows it falls out of their new framework

VC-1 classes generally: combined error tilde-O(sqrt(T)) recovered via a (3,1,1)-resilient function (Lemma 4.8 / Corollary 4.9) -- rate again attributable to Goel et al. 2024a, re-derived via the paper's framework

Finite inference dimension k (Kane et al. 2017 notion) implies combined error tilde-O(k T^{1-1/k}) via the paper's framework (Lemma 5.2, this paper's own instantiation of a prior combinatorial notion)
Halfspaces in R^2 have inference dimension 5 (fact from Kane et al. 2017, cited not derived), giving tilde-O(T^{4/5}) via Lemma 5.2

New: certificate dimension of R^2 halfspaces is 3 (this paper's Theorem 5.5), giving the first distribution-agnostic bound tilde-O(T^{2/3}) for this class -- sharper than the tilde-O(T^{4/5}) obtainable from inference dimension
Certificate dimension of axis-aligned rectangles is 2 (Remark 5.6, this paper)
baselines_compared: Goel et al. 2024a known-D upper bound O(d^2 log T) for VC dimension d (theoretical prior-work rate, not reproduced/beaten empirically -- used as the point of contrast for the paper's Omega(sqrt(T)) agnostic lower bound)

Goel et al. 2024a distribution-agnostic upper bounds tilde-O(sqrt(dT)) for VC-1 and axis-aligned rectangles (prior-work rates that this paper's lower bound matches / whose algorithms this paper's framework recovers as special cases)

Blum et al. 2021 result that R^2 halfspaces are not robustly learnable under clean-label poisoning without abstention (prior-work impossibility result contrasted with this paper's positive tilde-O(T^{2/3}) result once abstention is allowed)
